title: High-Yield Skeletal Muscle Protein Recovery from TRIzol® after RNA and DNA Extraction
author: Yuan Wen
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.bii3kcgn

steps.title: Mince muscle tissue into small (~10 mg) pieces
steps.instruction: Mince muscle tissue into small (~10 mg) pieces. Critical step: The large amount of sarcomeric proteins in skeletal muscle makes it difficult to solubilize; starting with small masses of muscle initially will increase protein yield.

steps.title: Isolate RNA using TRIzol® reagent, following manufacturer’s instructions
steps.instruction: Isolate RNA using TRIzol® reagent, following manufacturer’s instructions. Important point: Use 0.5 ml of TRIzol® reagent per piece (<15 mg) of tissue. Important point: For better phase separation, use 100 µl of BCP instead of Chloroform.

steps.title: Remove top RNA fraction (aqueous phase) according to manufacturer’s recommendation
steps.instruction: Remove top RNA fraction (aqueous phase) according to manufacturer’s recommendation.

steps.title: Follow manufacturer’s instructions for RNA precipitation,...
steps.instruction: Follow manufacturer’s instructions for RNA precipitation, wash, and re-suspension or choice of RNA column purification kit.

steps.title: Precipitate DNA following TRIzol® manufacturer’s instructions
steps.instruction: Precipitate DNA following TRIzol® manufacturer’s instructions. Critical Step: Use 150 µl of 100% EtOH and centrifuge at 5000g for 10 minutes.

steps.title: Wash and re-suspend DNA pellet according...
steps.instruction: Wash and re-suspend DNA pellet according to manufacturer’s instructions or choice of genomic DNA column purification kit.

steps.title: Transfer an aliquot (~0
steps.instruction: Transfer an aliquot (~0.45 ml) of protein-TRIzol®-EtOH solution into 2 ml tube. Important point: This type of tube is required due to large volume (~1.8 ml).

steps.title: Add 650 μl of 100% EtOH...
steps.instruction: Add 650 μl of 100% EtOH (protein precipitation step) to protein-containing phenol–ethanol supernatant.

steps.title: After vortexing the samples, mix with...
steps.instruction: After vortexing the samples, mix with 100 μl of BCP and vortex again.

steps.title: For phase separation, add 600 μl water and vortex vigorously
steps.instruction: For phase separation, add 600 μl water and vortex vigorously.

steps.title: Centrifuge at 12,000g for 5 minutes at room temperature
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at 12,000g for 5 minutes at room temperature.

steps.title: Remove and discard the upper aqueous...
steps.instruction: Remove and discard the upper aqueous layer (proteins are in the interphase).

steps.title: Add 700 μl of 100% EtOH...
steps.instruction: Add 700 μl of 100% EtOH to the remaining interphase and BCP phase.

steps.title: Vortex samples vigorously and centrifuge at...
steps.instruction: Vortex samples vigorously and centrifuge at 12,000g for 5 minutes to pellet the protein.

steps.title: Remove supernatant and dry the pellet...
steps.instruction: Remove supernatant and dry the pellet at room temperature for 1-2 minutes. Critical Step: The protein pellet should not be dried excessively.

steps.title: Add desired volume of SDS-Urea Buffer...
steps.instruction: Add desired volume of SDS-Urea Buffer (at least 100 μl per 10 mg tissue), vortex, and allow protein pellet to dissolve at room temperature. Important point: Make fresh buffer for optimal solubility.

steps.title: To quantify protein concentrations, follow the...
steps.instruction: To quantify protein concentrations, follow the manufacturer’s protocol for the Bio-Rad RC/DC Protein Assay (5000121). Important point: The RC component of the protein assay is necessary due to the presence of reducing agents in the SDS-urea buffer. Critical Step: Protein samples in the SDS-urea buffer are diluted 10-fold to reduce the total starting amounts of protein and reducing agents in the assay. Protein standards are made using bovine serum albumin solubilized in the respective buffer and are subjected to the same dilution step as the unknown samples ensure consistency.
